question: Explain the primary purpose of a Burndown Chart in an Agile project and list two key pieces of information it provides to the team.
expectedPoints: A Burndown Chart is a graphical tool used to track the amount of work remaining in a sprint (or release) against the time left to complete it.
It visualizes the project's progress, showing if the team is on track to complete the planned work by the sprint end date.
It provides the team with a clear view of the total work remaining (often in story points or ideal hours) and the project's current velocity trend.

question: Compare and contrast a Task Board and a Kanban Board. Describe the core components of a typical Kanban board and how it helps manage workflow.
expectedPoints: A Task Board is a simple board (physical or digital) used primarily in Scrum to track the progress of tasks within a single sprint, typically with columns like To Do, In Progress, and Done.

A Kanban Board is a tool from the Kanban methodology used for visualizing work, limiting work-in-progress (WIP), and maximizing efficiency (flow). It is continuous and not reset at the end of a fixed sprint.
Core components of a Kanban board include columns representing each stage of the workflow (e.g., Backlog, Ready, Development, Testing, Done) and WIP limits on each column to prevent bottlenecks.
It helps manage workflow by making blockers visible, highlighting inefficiencies, and promoting a continuous, pull-based system of work delivery.

question: Describe how an automated CI/CD pipeline acts as a foundational tool set for enabling Agile practices. Your answer should explain the concepts of Continuous Integration and Continuous Delivery and discuss at least three specific benefits they provide to an Agile team.
expectedPoints: Continuous Integration (CI) is the practice of automatically building and testing code every time a team member commits changes to version control. This ensures new code integrates smoothly and defects are caught early.
Continuous Delivery (CD) automates the process of deploying code changes to a staging or production environment after CI, making releases reliable, frequent, and low-risk.
It enables the Agile principle of rapid, iterative delivery by providing a fast and reliable feedback loop on the quality and deployability of the software.

Benefits include: (1) Reduced integration problems and faster feedback for developers, (2) Higher software quality and fewer bugs released to production due to automated testing, and (3) The ability to release value to customers frequently and predictably, supporting Agile's iterative nature.
It also reduces manual, error-prone deployment tasks, freeing the team to focus on adding value and fostering a culture of technical excellence, which is a key Agile principle.
